title: Near-Earth asteroids discovered over time
subtitle: Cumulative number of discovered asteroids with an orbit that brings them into proximity with Earth. This is broken down by size of the asteroid.
caption:
Near-Earth asteroids discovered over time

Cumulative number of discovered asteroids with an orbit that brings them into proximity with Earth. This is broken down by size of the asteroid.

Between 1990 and 2026, the cumulative count of discovered near-Earth asteroids—defined as those whose closest approach to the Sun is less than 1.3 astronomical units—rose from near zero to just over 40,000, with growth accelerating markedly after 2000. The stacked area chart divides discoveries into three size categories: smaller than 140 meters, between 140 meters and 1 kilometer, and larger than 1 kilometer. The largest category by far is asteroids smaller than 140 meters, which account for the bulk of the total and show the steepest rise, especially after 2010, reaching roughly 28,000 by 2026. The middle band, asteroids between 140 meters and 1 kilometer, grows more gradually but steadily, climbing to about 11,000 by the end of the period. The smallest group, asteroids larger than 1 kilometer, remains nearly flat throughout, hovering around 1,000 discoveries, indicating that most large objects in this class were already identified early in the observation window. The overall curve bends upward sharply after 2005, with the slope steepening further past 2015, showing that discovery rates increased substantially in the most recent decade. The vertical axis spans from 0 to 40,000, marked at intervals of 5,000, while the horizontal axis runs from 1990 to 2026 in five-year increments. The data shows that detection efforts have been increasingly effective at finding smaller bodies, while the population of kilometer-scale objects has remained relatively stable in cumulative count. The dominant visible pattern is the exponential-like growth in total discoveries, driven almost entirely by the accumulation of sub-140-meter asteroids.
